1: The opening summons — שִׁמְעוּ (shim'u, hear!) — calls Israel to covenant attention, the same imperative as the Shema (Deut 6:4). This anti-idol polemic (vv1-16) is one of the most sustained in the OT, parallel in many ways to Isa 40-48.
2: חֻקּוֹת (chuqqot, customs/practices) of the nations: the same root used for YHWH's covenant statutes, here applied to pagan religious practice. אוֹתוֹת (otot, signs) of the heavens — astral divination was central to Babylonian religion; Jeremiah directly targets what would be the dominant religious culture of exile.
3: הֶבֶל (hevel, breath/vapor/worthlessness) is the key verdict on idolatry throughout this polemic. The word is Qohelet's signature term (Ecclesiastes); here it renders idol-worship as existentially empty, not merely prohibited. The craftsman (charash) cuts a tree from the forest with an axe (garzen) — material and finite origins exposed from the start.
4: The idol is adorned with silver and gold, fastened with hammer and nails — a human artifact that cannot stand upright without being nailed down. The polemic anticipates Paul's diagnosis in Rom 1:23 (exchanging the glory of the immortal God for images made to look like mortal creatures).
5: Like a scarecrow (tomer, literally 'palm-trunk post') in a cucumber field — the idol cannot speak, cannot walk, must be carried. The comparison stresses functional uselessness: it occupies the field but produces nothing and controls nothing. 'Do not fear them' — the nations Israel might envy are worshiping what cannot act.
6: The contrast opens: מֵאֵין כָּמוֹךָ (me'eyn kamokha, there is none like you) — the incomparability formula. גָּדוֹל שִׁמְךָ בִּגְבוּרָה — YHWH's name (his character, his reputation) is great in power (gevurah).
7: מִי לֹא יִירָאֲךָ (mi lo yira'akha, who would not fear you?) — rhetorical question implying universal warrant for YHWH-reverence. Among all the wise of nations and all their kingdoms, none compares. כִּי לְךָ יָאָה (ki lekha ya'eh, for it is fitting for you) — the fear is appropriate, not merely coerced.
8: בָּעַר (ba'ar, brutish/stupid): a term for the irrationality of idol-worship, used three times in this chapter (vv8, 14, 21). Unlike hevel which stresses emptiness, ba'ar stresses acting below human rational capacity. The wooden idol-system is a teaching of hevel — a pedagogy of vapor.
9: Silver from תַּרְשִׁישׁ (Tarshish, the far west) and gold from אוּפָז (Uphaz, an unidentified gold source) — the idol is cosmopolitan in sourcing, international in pretension, and yet still a fabrication of human craftsmen (charash) and goldsmiths (tzoreph). Blue and purple cloth complete the royal appearance — but it is an appearance only.
10: וַיהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֱמֶת (veYHWH Elohim emet, but YHWH is the true/real God): emet is not merely factual correctness but covenantal reliability and deep ontological reality — the opposite of hevel. אֱלֹהִים חַיִּים (Elohim chayyim, living God): the idols are dead artifacts; YHWH is alive and self-existent. מֶלֶךְ עוֹלָם (melekh olam, eternal king): belonging to the age/eternity — the same phrase Jesus applies to the Father in John 17:3 ('the only true God').
11: This verse is unique in the Hebrew Bible: written entirely in Aramaic, not Hebrew — the only Aramaic verse in Jeremiah. The Aramaic is likely intentional, addressed to nations who would understand it: in their own language, their gods are condemned. Elahayya di shemayya ve-ar'a la avadu — the gods who did not make heaven and earth will perish (ye'avadu) from the earth and from under the heavens.
12: עֹשֵׂה אֶרֶץ בְּכֹחוֹ (oseh eretz bekocho): YHWH made the earth by his power (koach). Three parallel cola: power (koach), wisdom (chokhmah), understanding (tevunah). These are the same divine attributes Prov 3:19-20 names as instruments of creation — the wisdom-creation tradition that becomes Christological in 1 Cor 1:30 and Col 1:16.
13: YHWH's voice over the waters produces roaring heavens; clouds rise from the ends of the earth (aqtzei-aretz). Lightning (baraqim) for rain; wind (ruach) from his storehouses — YHWH is not a national deity but the lord of cosmic weather, precisely what the Baal cult claimed for its storm-god.
14: נִבְעַר כָּל-אָדָם מִדַּעַת (niv'ar kol-adam mida'at, every person is brutish without knowledge): the idol-maker's problem is epistemic, not technical. The goldsmith is shamed by his idol because it is a פֶּסֶל שֶׁקֶר (pesel sheqer, image of falsehood/deception): sheqer (falsehood) is the moral opposite of emet. No breath (ruach) is in them.
15: הֶבֶל הֵמָּה (hevel hemmah, they are vapor/vanity) — the final verdict. מַעֲשֵׂה תַּעְתֻּעִים (ma'aseh ta'tu'im, a work of mockery/delusion): will be destroyed in the time of their punishment (pekudatam).
16: לֹא כְאֵלֶּה חֵלֶק יַעֲקֹב (lo ke'elleh chelek Ya'akov): chelek (portion/lot/share) — YHWH is what Jacob possesses, not what Jacob makes. כִּי יוֹצֵר הַכֹּל הוּא (ki yotzer hakol hu, for he is the Maker of everything): the one who makes all things cannot be one of the things made. Israel is his נַחֲלָתוֹ (nachalato, his inheritance) — YHWH Tzeva'ot is his name.
17: The scene shifts to impending exile. כַּנְסִי (kansi, gather up): addressed to Jerusalem personified (feminine). Gather your bundle (keli) from the ground — the besieged city is told to prepare for deportation.
18: קֹלֵעַ (qola', a slinger): YHWH will fling (qela') the inhabitants as a slinger flings a stone. The violence of the image captures the force of exile — not a gradual departure but an expulsion. הַפַּעַם (hapa'am, at this time) — this time is definitive.
19: אוֹי לִי (oy li, woe to me): Jeremiah's lament breaks into the oracle. שִׁבְרִי (shivri) and מַכָּתִי (makati) — my fracture, my wound: Jeremiah identifies with Judah's coming trauma. נְחֵלִי (nechelil): 'This is truly my burden' — I will carry it (essa).
20: The lament continues with tent imagery (oholi, my tent; yeriyo'tai, my tent-cords): the covenant community as a tent whose cords are broken. בָּנַי יְצָאֻנִי (banai yetza'uni, my children have gone from me) — exile as family dissolution. There is no one to pitch the tent again.
21: הָרֹעִים בָּעֲרוּ (haro'im ba'aru, the shepherds acted brutishly): ba'ar again — the leaders who should guide the flock have become as irrational as idol-worshipers. They have not sought (darash) YHWH — the prophetic demand that leaders consult YHWH (Deut 12:5 uses the same root for seeking YHWH at the appointed place).
22: קוֹל שְׁמוּעָה (qol shemu'ah, sound of a report!) — the watchword of judgment from the north. The cities of Judah will become a lair of jackals (tannim) — the standard desolation image in Jeremiah (cf. 51:37).
23: יָדַעְתִּי יְהוָה כִּי לֹא לָאָדָם דַּרְכּוֹ (yada'ti YHWH ki lo la'adam darko, I know, O YHWH, that man's way is not his own): one of the most theologically freighted statements in the book. The prophet confesses that human beings cannot direct their own steps — a basis for the new covenant hope of ch 31 (inner transformation by YHWH). Cf. Prov 20:24; Phil 2:13 (God who works in you both to will and to act).
24: יַסְּרֵנִי יְהוָה אַךְ בְּמִשְׁפָּט (yassareni YHWH akh bemishpat, correct me, O YHWH, but in justice): Jeremiah asks not to escape chastisement but for it to be measured (bemishpat), not in anger (be'appekha). The fear: being reduced to nothing (lema'at).
25: The prayer turns outward: pour out your wrath on the nations who do not know you and on the families who do not call on your name — cf. Ps 79:6. Israel's judgment is not YHWH's final word; the nations who consumed Jacob will face reckoning.
